planting.note: Plant container-grown specimens in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic, humus-rich soil in partial shade in spring; Japanese snowbell dislikes drought and alkaline soil — provide moist, acidic, organically enriched soil and protection from hot afternoon sun in zones 7–8.

directions: Plant container-grown specimens in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic, humus-rich soil in partial shade; Japanese snowbell is one of the most exquisitely beautiful small flowering trees for the woodland garden — in May and June it produces thousands of small, nodding, bell-shaped, pure-white flowers with reflexed petals and golden anthers that dangle in rows beneath the spreading branches like a cascade of miniature lily-of-the-valley, releasing a pleasant, light fragrance. The tree is horizontal in branch habit, layered, and elegant in all seasons — the dense, dark-green summer foliage, the flower display, the attractive fall color, and the ridged gray bark create year-round interest. It performs beautifully as an understory specimen under tall oaks and as a focal point at the edge of a woodland garden. 'Snowfall' (extremely heavy-flowering), 'Pink Chimes' (pale-pink flowers), and 'Carillon' (weeping habit) are exceptional selections. Requires moist, acidic soil; absolutely does not tolerate alkalinity or drought.
sources: North Carolina State Extension — Plants Database

planting.note: Root semi-hardwood cuttings in summer or plant container-grown specimens in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, average soil in partial shade to full sun in spring or fall; Chinese holly tolerates heat, drought, and clay soils far better than English holly and is the primary holly of choice for Gulf Coast and southeastern landscapes.

directions: Root semi-hardwood cuttings in summer or plant container specimens in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, average soil in partial shade to full sun; Chinese holly is the most heat-tolerant and drought-tolerant of all the landscape hollies — an essential, tough, glossy-leaved evergreen shrub for Gulf Coast and southeastern landscapes that tolerates summer heat, poor soil, clay, drought, and partial shade far better than English holly (I. aquifolium). The leaves are rectangular with four characteristic spiny corners, giving them a distinctive, blocky appearance different from other hollies. Most cultivars set heavy crops of large, brilliant red berries without a pollinator tree. 'Burfordii' (single terminal spine, heavy berries, classic selection), 'Needlepoint' (narrow, single-spined leaf, columnar form), 'Dazzler' (heavy berry producer), and 'Rotunda' (compact, dwarf, no berries) are widely grown selections. Remarkably tough and long-lived once established in the landscape.
sources: Clemson Cooperative Extension Home & Garden Information Center

planting.note: Root semi-hardwood cuttings in summer or plant container-grown specimens in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, average soil in partial to full shade in spring; kerria is one of the most shade-tolerant flowering shrubs available, producing better bloom in partial shade than in full sun in hot climates.

directions: Root semi-hardwood cuttings or plant container specimens in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, average soil in partial to full shade; Japanese rose is one of the most valuable flowering shrubs for shade — producing brilliant golden-yellow, rose-like flowers in early spring on gracefully arching, bright-green stems that are themselves ornamental through winter when the bark retains vivid chartreuse-green color. It spreads gradually by suckering to form colonies and is excellent for naturalizing in shaded areas, under trees, and on north-facing slopes where little else blooms. The double-flowered form 'Pleniflora' (pompom-like, globe-shaped flowers) is more commonly grown than the single species. 'Golden Guinea' (single flowers, larger) and 'Picta' (white-variegated leaves) are other notable cultivars. Bloom is most abundant in partial shade in hot-summer climates; full sun can bleach and shorten the flower display. Tolerates considerable root competition from trees.
sources: Clemson Cooperative Extension Home & Garden Information Center
North Carolina State Extension — Plants Database

planting.note: Root stem-tip cuttings in summer or plant container-grown specimens in well-drained, average to fertile, slightly acidic to slightly alkaline soil in partial shade to full sun; box honeysuckle is extremely easy to root and establishes rapidly, making it one of the most economical shade hedging options available.

directions: Root stem-tip cuttings or plant container specimens in well-drained, average to fertile, slightly acidic to slightly alkaline soil in partial shade to full sun; box honeysuckle is a Chinese evergreen shrub grown as a boxwood alternative for formal hedging in shade and difficult conditions where true boxwood (Buxus) struggles — its small, paired, glossy, oval leaves and dense, twiggy growth habit create the same refined, fine-textured, formal effect that makes boxwood so desirable, but it tolerates more shade, heat, drought, and alkaline soil than boxwood. Small, creamy-white, fragrant flowers appear in spring, followed by small, translucent, purple-red berries. It clips beautifully into formal geometric shapes and responds vigorously to shearing. 'Elegant' and 'Maigrün' (Golden-tipped new growth) are improved selections. An underused, extremely tough alternative to the blight-susceptible boxwoods increasingly being replaced across North American gardens.
sources: Clemson Cooperative Extension Home & Garden Information Center

planting.note: Plant container-grown specimens in moist, well-drained, strongly acidic, humus-rich soil in partial shade in spring; sourwood resents transplanting once established and requires strongly acidic soil (pH 4.5–5.5) — alkaline soil causes rapid, permanent decline. Avoid disturbing the root zone once planted.

directions: Plant container-grown specimens in moist, well-drained, strongly acidic, humus-rich soil in partial shade; sourwood is a native American forest tree considered by many horticulturalists to be the finest small to medium-sized ornamental tree native to the eastern United States, offering four seasons of distinction: long, drooping panicles of small, white, lily-of-the-valley-like flowers in July and August (a rare native tree that flowers in midsummer when few other trees bloom); spectacular fall foliage in fiery scarlet-red to deep crimson — among the earliest and most vivid trees to color in autumn; persistent, creamy-white seed capsules that decorate the bare branches through winter; and elegant, slightly drooping branch tips that give the tree a graceful, somewhat weeping quality year-round. Sourwood honey (made by bees collecting nectar from the flowers) is considered among the finest American honeys. Strictly requires strongly acidic soil (pH 4.5–5.5) and declines rapidly if planted in alkaline or compacted soils. Resents root disturbance after planting.
sources: North Carolina State Extension — Plants Database
